lessons.title: The IPv4 Header, Field by Field
lessons.content:
IP is the **thin waist** of the Internet: everything rides over IP, and IP rides over everything. The IPv4 header is normally 20 bytes (no options) and you should be able to read it from a hex dump the way you once read a radar pulse's framing bits.

Walking the header top to bottom:

- **Version (4 bits)** = 4 for IPv4.
- **IHL (4 bits)** = header length in 32-bit words. Minimum value 5 (20 bytes); larger only when **options** are present.
- **DSCP/ECN (8 bits)** = the old Type of Service byte. The top 6 bits (DSCP) mark QoS class; the low 2 bits (ECN) let routers signal congestion *without dropping*.
- **Total Length (16 bits)** = entire datagram (header + payload) in bytes; caps a single datagram at 65,535.
- **Identification / Flags / Fragment Offset** = the fragmentation machinery (next lesson). Flags include **DF** (Don't Fragment) and **MF** (More Fragments).
- **TTL (8 bits)** = decremented at every hop; hits zero and the packet is discarded with an ICMP message. This is what stops L3 routing loops.
- **Protocol (8 bits)** = what's inside: 1=ICMP, 6=TCP, 17=UDP.
- **Header Checksum (16 bits)** = covers the header *only*; because TTL changes each hop, every router recomputes it.
- **Source / Destination Address (32 bits each)**.
- **Options + padding** = rare, security/diagnostics; their presence is why IHL exists.

Note what is **not** here: no sequence numbers, no acknowledgements, no ports. IP is connectionless and best-effort. Reliability and process addressing are the transport layer's job.

items.front: How long is an IPv4 header with no options, and what field tells you its length?
items.back: 20 bytes with no options. The IHL (Internet Header Length) field gives the length in 32-bit words: minimum 5 (5 x 4 = 20 bytes). IHL > 5 means options are present (up to 60 bytes total).

items.front: What does the Protocol field identify, and give three common values.
items.back: It identifies the encapsulated upper-layer payload so the receiver knows how to demultiplex it. Common values: 1 = ICMP, 6 = TCP, 17 = UDP.
items.hint: It points at the next header up the stack.

items.front: The IPv4 header checksum covers what, and why must every router recompute it?
items.back: It covers the header only, not the payload. Because TTL is decremented at every hop, the header changes, so each router must recompute the checksum. (TCP/UDP carry their own checksums covering the data.)

items.front: What is the DSCP/ECN byte, and what was it formerly called?
items.back: Formerly the Type of Service (ToS) byte. The high 6 bits are DSCP (Differentiated Services Code Point) for QoS classification; the low 2 bits are ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification), letting routers signal congestion by marking packets instead of dropping them.

items.prompt: An IPv4 header's first byte is 0x45. What does this tell you?
choices: Version 4, IHL 5 (a 20-byte header with no options)
Version 5, with DSCP set to expedited forwarding
TTL of 69 hops remaining
Protocol 69, an experimental transport
items.explanation: The high nibble (4) is the Version and the low nibble (5) is the IHL. IHL=5 means 5 x 32-bit words = 20 bytes, i.e. no options. 0x45 is the canonical first byte of an ordinary IPv4 packet.

items.prompt: Which of these is NOT carried in the IPv4 header?
choices: Source port number
Time To Live
Protocol number
Destination address
items.explanation: Ports live in the transport header (TCP/UDP), not in IP. IP addresses hosts; ports address processes. This separation is exactly the connectionless, best-effort nature of IP: it has no notion of a connection or a process endpoint.

items.prompt: What is the maximum value of the Total Length field, and what does it bound?
choices: 65,535 bytes, the size of a single IP datagram (header + payload)
1,500 bytes, the Ethernet MTU
1,460 bytes, the TCP maximum segment size
4,294,967,295 bytes, since it is a 32-bit counter
items.explanation: Total Length is 16 bits, so the maximum datagram is 2^16 - 1 = 65,535 bytes including the header. The 1,500-byte limit is the link-layer MTU, a separate constraint that drives fragmentation.

items.prompt: Why does IP provide no acknowledgements, sequence numbers, or retransmission?
choices: IP is connectionless and best-effort by design; reliability is delegated to the transport layer (TCP) or the application
Those features were removed in IPv4 to save header space and never existed
Routers strip them to speed up forwarding
They are present but only used when the DF flag is set
items.explanation: IP deliberately offers only best-effort, connectionless delivery: it will try to deliver a datagram but makes no guarantees about ordering, duplication, or loss. The end-to-end principle pushes reliability to the edges (TCP), keeping the network core simple and fast.

lessons.title: Fragmentation, MTU & Path MTU Discovery
lessons.content:
Every link has a **Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU)** — the largest payload its frames can carry. Classic Ethernet is 1500 bytes. When an IP datagram is larger than the next link's MTU, IPv4 can **fragment** it.

Fragmentation uses three header fields together:

- **Identification** — all fragments of one original datagram share this value, so the destination can group them.
- **Flags** — **MF** (More Fragments) is set on every fragment except the last; **DF** (Don't Fragment) forbids fragmentation entirely.
- **Fragment Offset** — where this fragment's data sits in the original, measured in 8-byte units.

Reassembly happens **only at the final destination**, never at intermediate routers, and if any fragment is lost the whole datagram is dropped. Fragmentation is therefore avoided today: it hurts performance and is a security headache.

The modern alternative is **Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD)**: the sender sets **DF=1** and sends a large packet. If a router along the path has a smaller MTU, it drops the packet and returns an **ICMP "Fragmentation Needed"** message stating the link MTU. The sender shrinks its packets and retries.

PMTUD's notorious failure is the **PMTU black hole**: a misconfigured firewall silently blocks the ICMP Fragmentation-Needed messages. The sender never learns to shrink, keeps sending oversized DF packets that keep getting dropped, and the connection stalls. This is endemic in **VPNs and tunnels**, where encapsulation overhead shrinks the effective MTU. The classic symptom — small packets (ping, SSH login) work but large transfers (file copy, web pages) hang — is the giveaway that you are chasing a PMTU/fragmentation problem, not a routing or DNS problem.

items.front: What is an MTU, and what is the typical Ethernet value?
items.back: Maximum Transmission Unit: the largest payload a link's frame can carry. Standard Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes. A datagram larger than the next-hop MTU must be fragmented or rejected (if DF is set).
items.hint: Largest payload one frame can carry.

items.front: Which three IPv4 fields cooperate to perform fragmentation and reassembly?
items.back: Identification (shared by all fragments of one datagram), Flags (MF = More Fragments on all but the last; DF = Don't Fragment), and Fragment Offset (position in the original, in 8-byte units).
items.hint: ID groups them, offset orders them, MF marks the end.

items.front: Where are IP fragments reassembled, and what happens if one is lost?
items.back: Only at the final destination host — never at intermediate routers. If any single fragment is lost, the destination cannot reassemble and discards the entire datagram, forcing the upper layer to resend all of it.

items.front: How does Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD) work?
items.back: The sender sets DF=1 on packets. A router whose outgoing link is too small drops the packet and replies with ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (Destination Unreachable: Fragmentation Needed), reporting the next-hop MTU. The sender lowers its packet size and retries, converging on the smallest MTU along the path.
items.hint: DF=1 plus an ICMP that reports the link MTU.

items.prompt: Ping to a server succeeds and SSH logins work, but large file transfers and many web pages stall completely. What is the most likely cause?
choices: A Path MTU / fragmentation problem (likely a PMTUD black hole on a tunnel or VPN)
The destination's DNS records are stale
TTL is set too low to reach the server
The routing table is missing a default route
items.explanation: Small packets fit any MTU and pass fine, so reachability (ping, login handshake) works. Large packets exceed a smaller path MTU; with DF set they are dropped, and if the ICMP Fragmentation-Needed messages are filtered the sender never shrinks them — a PMTU black hole. This pattern is the textbook signature of an MTU/fragmentation issue, common with VPNs and tunnels.

choices: A firewall silently drops the ICMP Fragmentation-Needed messages, so the sender never learns to shrink its DF packets and they keep being discarded
A router with a routing loop swallows packets until TTL expires
An IP address range that is unrouteable on the public Internet
A buffer overflow that consumes oversized datagrams
items.explanation: PMTUD depends on ICMP feedback. If a misconfigured firewall blocks the ICMP Type 3 Code 4 messages, the sender never receives the signal to reduce packet size; its oversized DF packets are dropped at the bottleneck link with no error returned, so large transfers hang indefinitely.

items.prompt: A datagram arrives at a router whose outbound link MTU is smaller than the packet, and the packet has DF=1. What does the router do?
choices: Drop the packet and send back ICMP Destination Unreachable: Fragmentation Needed
Fragment it anyway and clear the DF bit
Buffer it until a larger-MTU path becomes available
Forward it unchanged and let the next hop deal with it
items.explanation: DF=1 forbids fragmentation. The router cannot shrink the packet, so it discards it and returns ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (Fragmentation Needed) reporting the link MTU. This is exactly the feedback PMTUD relies on; if it is blocked, you get a black hole.

items.prompt: Why do VPNs and tunnels so often trigger MTU problems?
choices: Encapsulation adds header overhead, reducing the effective MTU below 1500, so packets sized for plain Ethernet no longer fit
Tunnels disable the TTL field, allowing infinite-size packets
VPN encryption increases the Total Length field beyond 65,535 bytes
Tunnels always use a fixed 576-byte MTU mandated by IPv4
items.explanation: A tunnel wraps each original packet in additional outer headers (e.g. IPsec, GRE, WireGuard). Those bytes eat into the 1500-byte budget, so the inner effective MTU drops (often to ~1400). Packets sized for 1500 with DF set then fail to traverse the tunnel, and if PMTUD's ICMP is blocked the transfer black-holes.

lessons.title: TTL, ICMP & the Forwarding/Routing Split
lessons.content:
**TTL** (Time To Live) is decremented by one at every router. When it reaches zero the packet is discarded and the router sends back **ICMP Time Exceeded**. This is what prevents L3 routing loops — contrast Ethernet at L2, which has *no* hop counter and relies on Spanning Tree to break loops.

**ICMP** is IP's signaling companion (protocol 1). Key messages:
- **Echo Request / Echo Reply** — `ping`; reachability and RTT.
- **Time Exceeded** — TTL hit zero; the engine behind **traceroute**.
- **Destination Unreachable** — no route, host down, or port closed.
- **Fragmentation Needed** (a Destination-Unreachable code) — PMTUD's feedback.
- **Redirect** — a router telling a host a better next hop exists.

**Traceroute** exploits TTL: send a packet with TTL=1, the first router decrements it to 0 and returns Time Exceeded (revealing hop 1); TTL=2 reveals hop 2; and so on, mapping the path hop by hop.

The **forwarding decision** at each router is **longest-prefix match**: the destination IP is compared against every routing-table prefix, and the entry with the most specific (longest) matching prefix wins. A `/0` default route is the fallback when nothing more specific matches.

Finally, the architectural distinction the whole modern stack builds on: **forwarding** is the per-packet **data plane** — fast, local, "which interface does this packet leave by?" **Routing** is the **control plane** — building and maintaining the table via protocols like OSPF and BGP. Forwarding consults the table; routing populates it. Separating the two is the conceptual seed of SDN.

items.front: How does TTL prevent routing loops at L3, and why does Ethernet need a different mechanism?
items.back: Each router decrements TTL by 1; at zero the packet is dropped (and ICMP Time Exceeded is sent), so a looping packet eventually dies. Ethernet frames have no hop counter, so an L2 loop would circulate forever — which is why switches need Spanning Tree Protocol to break loops.
items.hint: L3 has a counter; L2 needs STP.

items.front: How does traceroute use TTL and ICMP to map a path?
items.back: It sends probes with increasing TTL: TTL=1 expires at hop 1, which returns ICMP Time Exceeded (revealing that router); TTL=2 reveals hop 2; and so on. Each Time Exceeded message identifies the router where the packet died, building the path hop by hop.
items.hint: Increment TTL, collect each Time Exceeded.

items.front: Name the ICMP messages behind ping, traceroute, and PMTUD.
items.back: Ping = Echo Request / Echo Reply. Traceroute = Time Exceeded (TTL reached 0). PMTUD = Destination Unreachable, code 'Fragmentation Needed'. Other notables: Destination Unreachable (no route/host/port) and Redirect (better next hop).

The forwarding rule: a router compares the destination IP against all routing-table prefixes and selects the entry with the most specific (longest) matching prefix. A /24 beats a /16 beats the /0 default route. It guarantees a single, deterministic next hop.

items.prompt: A routing table has entries for 10.0.0.0/8, 10.1.0.0/16, 10.1.2.0/24, and 0.0.0.0/0. A packet is destined for 10.1.2.50. Which entry is used?
choices: 10.1.2.0/24 — the longest (most specific) matching prefix
0.0.0.0/0 — the default route always wins
10.0.0.0/8 — the first matching entry in the table
10.1.0.0/16 — the entry with the median prefix length
items.explanation: 10.1.2.50 matches all four prefixes, but forwarding uses longest-prefix match: the /24 is the most specific match and wins. Table order is irrelevant; only prefix length decides. The /0 default is consulted only when nothing more specific matches.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes forwarding from routing?
choices: Forwarding is the per-packet data-plane action of choosing an output interface; routing is the control-plane process of building the table (OSPF, BGP)
Forwarding builds the routing table; routing moves individual packets
Forwarding happens only on hosts; routing happens only on routers
They are two names for the same per-packet operation
items.explanation: Forwarding (data plane) is the fast, per-packet lookup that sends each packet out the right interface. Routing (control plane) runs protocols like OSPF and BGP to compute and maintain the table that forwarding consults. This separation is exactly what SDN centralizes and generalizes.

items.prompt: You ping a host and receive 'ICMP Time Exceeded' replies instead of Echo Replies. What does that indicate?
choices: Packets are expiring in transit (TTL reaching zero before arrival), often a routing loop or a too-small TTL
The destination host is up and responding normally
The destination port is closed
Path MTU is too small for the echo request
items.explanation: Time Exceeded means TTL hit zero before the packet reached the destination. That points to packets dying in transit — commonly a routing loop bouncing the packet until TTL runs out, or an artificially low initial TTL. It is distinct from Destination Unreachable (no route/host/port) and from Echo Reply (success).

items.prompt: What is the purpose of an ICMP Redirect message?
choices: A router informs a host on the same subnet that a better next-hop router exists for a given destination
A router tells the sender to fragment its packets
It reverses the source and destination addresses for return traffic
It forces the host to lower its TTL value
items.explanation: ICMP Redirect lets a router tell a directly-connected host, 'for that destination, send to this other gateway on our shared subnet instead of through me.' It optimizes the host's next-hop choice. (It is often disabled today for security, since forged redirects can be used to reroute traffic.)
